steps.title: Price each class under the promotion
steps.content:
With a regular price of \$26.40:
  • Class 1 is free: \$0.
  • Class 2 is half off: $\dfrac{26.40}{2}=\$13.20$.
  • Each remaining class (classes 3 through $x$) costs the full \$26.40. The number of those classes is $x-2$.

steps.title: Add the costs into a single expression
Compare to each choice
steps.content:
Check each option against $26.40(x-2)+13.20$:
  • A) $26.40(x-1)+13.20$ charges full price for $x-1$ classes, which would also charge full price for class 2 — eliminate.
  • B) $26.40(x-2)+13.20$ matches the derived expression.
  • C) $26.40(x-1)+13.20(x-2)$ overcharges full price and adds extra half-price terms — eliminate.
  • D) $26.40(x-2)+13.20(x-1)$ has too many half-price classes (should be exactly one) — eliminate.
That matches choice B — B is correct.
